title: Chicken casserole with cider and tarragon
subtitle: Chicken thighs braised in dry cider with cream and fresh tarragon
excerpt: Chicken thighs braised in dry cider with shallots and garlic, finished with double cream and fresh tarragon. A West Country and Norman French tradition — apple orchards produce the cider, and the result is lighter and more fragrant than a red wine braise. Serve with crusty bread or boiled new potatoes.
sourceNotes: Chicken braised in cider is a technique shared between the West Country of England and Normandy in France, where apple orchards are central to the agricultural landscape. The French poulet à la normande is a close relation. The British version is typically simpler — cider, cream, and tarragon rather than a full Norman preparation with Calvados and multiple cream additions.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a day ahead without the cream and tarragon. Reheat gently, then add the cream and fresh tarragon just before serving.

content.text: Use dry cider rather than sweet — the cream provides enough sweetness, and sweet cider produces a cloying sauce. Bone-in thighs hold their texture better than boneless over the braise time and produce more flavour into the sauce.

Add the tarragon at the very end, off the heat. Tarragon is one of the herbs that loses its character most dramatically when cooked — the anise flavour that defines it disappears with heat. Stir it in just before serving.

Season the {{chicken-thigh}} with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide casserole over medium-high heat. Brown the chicken skin-side down for 6 minutes until deeply golden. Turn and cook for 2 minutes more. Remove and set aside. Add the {{shallot}} and {{garlic}} to the pan and cook for 3 minutes until softened. Pour in the {{cider-dry}} and scrape up any browned bits. Return the chicken, skin-side up. Simmer partially covered for 35 minutes, until the chicken is cooked through and the cider has reduced by about a third. Stir in the {{double-cream}} and {{dijon-mustard}}. Simmer for 5 minutes until the sauce coats the back of a spoon. Remove from the heat. Stir in the {{tarragon}}. Taste and adjust seasoning.

items.symptom: Sauce very thin even after 35 minutes of simmering
items.cause: Too much cider, or lid on during the braise
items.fix: Simmer uncovered for the full time to allow reduction. If still thin after adding the cream, remove the chicken and reduce the sauce further before returning it

items.cause: Added too early and cooked through
items.fix: Tarragon must go in off the heat, just before serving. It loses its anise character within minutes of cooking

content.text: Chicken with cider and tarragon is associated with the apple-growing counties of Devon, Somerset, and Herefordshire, where cider is produced in quantity. It is an autumn dish, when new season's cider is available and the year begins to turn cooler. The French Norman version is better known internationally, but the British West Country tradition is older.
